text.title: Solo Self Code Review Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for self-reviewing your own (or an AI agent's) PR when no human peer is available.

rule.statement: Every PR is reviewed against a written, version-controlled self-review checklist of at least 10 items (covers: tests cover new branches, no print/console.log left in, error handling on external calls, no hard-coded secrets, migration is reversible, feature flag exists for user-visible changes, etc.). Each item is ticked or explicitly waived in the PR description.
rule.rationale: Without a written checklist, the self-reviewer's attention drifts toward whatever felt risky while coding — missing exactly the regressions outside that focus.

rule.statement: Before merge, the diff (or the full PR) is fed to an AI assistant with a fixed prompt that asks for: bugs, edge cases missed, security exposures, test gaps, and "what would a senior engineer flag here". The AI's response is pasted into the PR as a review comment. Self-review with NO AI second-reviewer is rejected.
rule.rationale: AI second-review catches a different class of issue than human self-review (especially edge-case enumeration). Treating it as mandatory institutionalises the distance the operator can't get alone.

rule.statement: The repo has a written list of "risk-flag" path/file patterns: DB migrations, billing / payments code, auth / session handling, hard delete operations, infrastructure config. A PR touching any flagged path is marked RISKY in its title and triggers r4.
rule.rationale: Explicit risk flags catch the moment the operator's risk-perception fails ("it's just a small migration"). The list survives optimism.

rule.statement: RISKY PRs (per r3) MUST sit at least 24 hours between "ready for review" and merge. During the cool-off the operator re-reads the diff at least once with fresh eyes. Emergency overrides require an open incident or written exception in the PR.
rule.rationale: Solo operators systematically over-trust freshly-written code. A mandatory 24h pause produces a fresh re-read that catches the obvious bugs the original author missed.

rule.statement: CI (tests + linter + typecheck) MUST be green at the SHA being merged, with no skipped or `xit`-marked tests added in the same PR. If CI fails post-merge on main, revert within 30 minutes — fix-forward is forbidden unless the build was green at merge time and the failure is environmental.
rule.rationale: Solo merges have no second pair of eyes to catch a yellow CI. "Fix-forward" turns one bad merge into a multi-hour bisect later; revert-first preserves the ability to ship anything else that day.

example.code:
## PR #142 — Migrate user.email to citext (RISKY)
### Self-review checklist (10/10) - [x] tests added for case-insensitive lookups - [x] migration is reversible (down() implemented + tested) - [x] no secrets / no print - [x] feature flag: n/a (DB-only, no user-visible change) - [x] ... (remaining 6)
### AI second-reviewer notes > Risk: existing unique index on email is case-sensitive. If two rows differ > only by case, the citext conversion will fail. Suggest pre-check query in > migration. ACTION: pre-check query added in commit a3f1.
### Risk flags hit: db_migration Ready: 2026-05-19 11:00 UTC. Earliest merge: 2026-05-20 11:00 UTC (24h cool-off). CI: green at a3f1c8d.
example.note: Checklist passed, AI second-reviewer caught the case-collision risk, cool-off observed, CI green. Five rules satisfied.

antipattern.description: Founder writes a migration, runs tests locally, merges 12 minutes later because "it's just a column rename". Migration fails in production due to unrelated FK constraint at 2 AM.
antipattern.reason: Violates r3 (migration is risk-flagged), r4 (no cool-off), and skipped AI second-reviewer (r2). Each rule individually would have caught it.
